bibliographic.citation: J. W. H. Atkins, ed., The Owl and the Nightingale (Cambridge: University Press, 1922), xc + 231 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Owl and the Nightingale

entities.description: J. W. H. Atkins's edition, printing independently examined Cotton and Jesus texts on facing pages with introduction, notes, translation, and glossary.
entities.label: Atkins 1922 Cambridge edition
notes: Jesus text J occupies odd printed pp. 3–151; Cotton text C occupies even pp. 2–150.
The separately modeled Internet Archive scan is a textual surrogate and comparison source. It is not established as the exact carrier used during electronic keying.
John Stevenson's 1838 Roxburghe Club edition predates Atkins and was based principally on Cotton with some Jesus readings; Atkins is not described as the poem's first publication.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1922. Internet Archive's exact item metadata marks possible-copyright-status as NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for region US and records the scanning review's finding of no visible copyright notice; current Copyright Office duration guidance independently places U.S.-published works from 1922 in the public domain.
rights.component: Atkins's 1922 published edition, separate from the modern Internet Archive scan and delivery layer

access.accessMethod: Consult the current Western Medieval Manuscripts catalog record.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Jesus College MS 29, Part II

access.accessMethod: Contact the Jesus College librarian for physical-manuscript or reproduction questions beyond the public facsimile.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Jesus College MS 29, Part II

entities.description: The trilingual poetic-anthology component of a composite codex and the manuscript witness transcribed for OwlJ.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian describes Part I as Polychronicon, ff. 1r–143bv, and Part II as the trilingual anthology, ff. 144r–257v.

entities.label: Oxford, Jesus College MS 29, Part II

physicalDescription.extent: Composite codex: ff. ii + 144 + 114 + ii; poem at current ff. 156ra–168vb
physicalDescription.format: Two-column manuscript text in Part II
notes: The complete current digital object has 532 canvases.
Current f. 156r retains the older pencil folio number 229, establishing the legacy/current crosswalk.
The Jesus scribe adapted and modernized the shared text more freely than the Cotton scribe.

rights.basis: The underlying medieval text and manuscript writing date from the thirteenth century; this statement does not cover modern photographs or catalog descriptions.
rights.component: Medieval manuscript text of The Owl and the Nightingale

access.accessMethod: No physical object or reproduction exists for this reconstructed lost textual node.
access.entityLabel: Reconstructed lost common exemplar X

entities.description: The lost common textual exemplar reconstructed from physical and textual correspondences between Jesus and Cotton. It is a transmission hypothesis, not the authorial original and not an extant cataloged manuscript.
entities.label: Reconstructed lost common exemplar X
notes: Current scholarship supports independent copying of both surviving manuscripts from X.
No shelfmark, holding repository, public reproduction, or secure copying date exists for this reconstructed node.

access.accessMethod: No physical manuscript, shelfmark, facsimile, transcription, or known reproduction was established; the consulted scholarly record preserves only a reported lost-witness notice.
access.entityLabel: Reported lost manuscript of ‘De conflictu inter philomenam et bubonem in anglicis’

entities.description: A separately reported lost manuscript that Ker 1963 considered likely to have contained The Owl and the Nightingale. The identification is preserved as a qualified report, not as an extant witness or as the reconstructed common exemplar X.
entities.label: Reported lost manuscript of ‘De conflictu inter philomenam et bubonem in anglicis’
notes: No shelfmark, holding repository, facsimile, transcription, or secure relation to X was established from the consulted public source.

next_steps: Consult Cartlidge's full dating argument and subsequent scholarship cited by Fein and PCMEP.
Keep manuscript attestation dates separate from the unresolved date of intellectual composition.
Treat the normalized span as a discovery envelope, not a claim of continuous composition or settled endpoints.
openQuestions.question: Can the broad 1175–1285 discovery envelope be narrowed among the early, traditional 1189–1216, and later-thirteenth-century/post-1272 proposals?

next_steps: Review the competing Nicholas-of-Guildford and female-authorship arguments cited by Fein.
Retain Nicholas only as a disputed candidate unless external evidence resolves the internal-reference problem.
openQuestions.question: Can the internal references to Nicholas of Guildford support an authorial attribution, or must the work remain anonymous?

next_steps: Borrow and inspect the controlled 1971 scan for carrier-specific matter or resetting.
Seek OTA deposit history, production documentation, or a copy-specific source statement.
Compare representative 1971 pages against the public 1922 scan without treating textual agreement alone as carrier proof.
openQuestions.question: Was OTA item 1684 keyed from a specific 1971 Russell & Russell copy, or does its source line merely combine the 1922 textual edition date with the later reprint publisher?

next_steps: Recover any older OTA deposit or conversion documentation that predates the current SGML component.
Preserve the verified fact that the anomaly is already present in OTA and survives in later U-M/MED wrappers.
Use the Atkins scan to keep the five strings classified as marginal navigation rather than verse or physical lacunae.
openQuestions.question: At what production stage were five marginal folio-column labels misclassified as poem-line text?

next_steps: Inspect Ker 1963, introduction p. ix n. 4, and the catalog or inventory source underlying his notice.
Do not merge the reported lost item with X unless physical or textual evidence establishes that identity.
openQuestions.question: Can the reported lost manuscript titled ‘De conflictu inter philomenam et bubonem in anglicis’ be identified more securely or placed in relation to the reconstructed exemplar X?

next_steps: Check EETS and additional holding libraries for a provider-authorized digital surrogate.
Use the complete current institutional manuscript facsimiles for visual research in the meantime.
notes: This date-bounded negative does not prove that no private, subscription, or unindexed copy exists.
openQuestions.question: Is there a lawful complete public digital copy of Ker's 1963 EETS facsimile?

next_steps: Obtain a written determination from British Library Licensing for the intended jurisdiction and publication context.
Retain the exact item-level statement ‘Public Domain in most countries other than the UK’ without generalizing it worldwide.
Keep local research caching separate from authorization to redistribute the image bundle.
openQuestions.question: What permission or provider terms apply to UK, repository-publication, print-on-demand, or commercial reuse of the British Library Cotton facsimile images?

next_steps: Request written clarification from the University of Michigan Library/Middle English Compendium about redistribution and commercial reuse of the legacy-derived raw OwlJ encoding, citing the exact pinned file and current download statement.
Ask the Kyvero-Press/CME maintainer whether the repository Unlicense was intended to cover inherited corpus XML data and retained third-party notices.

Until clarified, keep the pinned repository artifact, current U-M presentation, current MED XML, OTA deposit, scholarly editions, and manuscript-image rights as separate layers and avoid treating one layer's statement as blanket permission for all others.
openQuestions.question: Do the current U-M/MED no-copyright and unrestricted-reuse statements, or the Kyvero-Press/CME root Unlicense, resolve or supersede the pinned OwlJ artifact's retained legacy personal-scholarly-use-only notice for redistribution of this exact XML?

assertion.notes: The pinned wrapper differs from current distributions, but the title, source statement, rubric, all 1,794 poetic lines, and locator anomaly correspond.

reviewNotes: The cataloged text is the complete Jesus-witness version; the abstract poem, Cotton version, editions, encodings, repository artifact, and image objects remain distinct graph layers.
Current manuscript foliation is used for direct citation. Atkins, OTA, and CME legacy foliation is crosswalked by subtracting 73: old 229r–241v equals current 156r–168v.

The repository BODY has 47 folio MILESTONE elements plus five locator strings embedded literally in L text. The same five labels are unstructured in current OTA SGML and current MEDictionary XML; the 1922 scan shows them as marginal edition navigation, not verse.
The cached Cambridge 1922 scan is a complete public textual surrogate and comparison source, not proof that it was the exact physical carrier keyed by OTA.

Rights statements are scoped to the named entity and component. Medieval text, modern edition, digital encoding, manuscript image, catalog data, and repository file rights do not inherit automatically across relations.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a wrapper/version of University of Michigan CME item OwlJ, a Jesus College MS 29 encoding whose 1,794 poetic line strings match the current OTA 1684 OwlJ component and current MEDictionary raw XML. All identify J. W. H. Atkins's edition as their textual source, but the OTA statement combines a 1922 date with New York publisher Russell & Russell, strongly suggesting the 1971 reprint carrier while U-M cites the underlying Cambridge 1922 edition. Atkins independently transcribed the Jesus and Cotton witnesses on facing pages. Current scholarship reconstructs both manuscripts as independent descendants of a lost common exemplar X; Cotton is therefore a collateral witness, not an ancestor of OwlJ. Complete current facsimiles of both physical codices are publicly viewable and cached locally with exact work portions.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composition unresolved, approximately 1175–1285; the surviving Jesus witness was copied by c. 1300
catalogSummary.region: Southern England is the broad conventional localization; the Jesus witness's scribal language is localized to the Ledbury area of Herefordshire
catalogSummary.title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)

catalogingSubject.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete 1,794-line Middle English version transmitted in Oxford, Jesus College MS 29 (Part II), ff. 156ra–168vb, represented by the corpus item OwlJ. This witness-specific textual version is distinct from the abstract two-witness work, the collateral Cotton Caligula A IX version, Atkins's 1922 facing-page edition, the OTA and University of Michigan encodings, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)

locators.value: BODY HEAD rubric followed by 1,794 positional L elements; 18 supplied DIV1 groups

notes: The current foliation is used for manuscript citation; legacy foliation is retained as a crosswalk because it appears in Atkins, OTA, and the XML.
contentParts.summary: The complete Jesus-witness poem from its opening summer-valley scene through the disputants' unresolved journey toward Nicholas of Guildford.

assertion.notes: The normalized span is an intentionally broad discovery range encompassing current competing proposals, not a claim that composition continued across the interval.

notes: The 1190–1210 date inherited by the U-M/OTA headers is one historical position, not settled current consensus.
Proposals include c. 1175–1200, the traditional 1189–1216 or early-thirteenth-century range, and a later-thirteenth-century or post-1272 date.

assertion.notes: Both surviving copies are likely post-1272. This is an attestation boundary, not a composition date or a date assigned to the reconstructed common exemplar.

extent.description: One complete verse work comprising 1,794 lines, conventionally 897 rhyming couplets. The repository BODY contains 1,794 nonempty L elements for poem lines, plus a separate Latin HEAD rubric, but no LG elements; 897 is therefore an intellectual couplet count supported by the edition and DIMEV, not an XML wrapper count. L/@N appears only at every fifth canonical line and ends at 1790, so line extent is positional rather than a count of consecutively numbered attributes. The 18 supplied DIV1 wrappers and 47 MILESTONE elements are representation metrics; five further legacy folio-column labels are literal line text and are not physical gaps.

formStatements.description: A 1,794-line poem in predominantly octosyllabic rhyming couplets.

assertion.notes: Satiric style is a grounded secondary genre feature; it does not reduce the work's multivalent debate to a single satirical target.

notes: The record deliberately catalogs the Jesus-witness version because OwlJ extracts that witness alone from Atkins's facing-page edition; OwlC is the separately encoded Cotton counterpart.
The reconstructed common exemplar X is a textual hypothesis shared by the two witnesses, not the authorial original and not an extant manuscript.
Current foliation is used for direct manuscript citation. Atkins/OTA/XML legacy foliation is retained as an explicit crosswalk because current f. 156r bears the old pencil number 229.
The source XML's profile date 1190–1210 and the former Nicholas attribution are recorded as historical claims rather than silently promoted to current consensus.

next_steps: Consult the full arguments and bibliography in Fein 2022, especially the competing historical allusions.
Treat 1175–1285 as a broad discovery envelope rather than a settled date, and seek evidence that can narrow the competing proposals.
Do not elevate the U-M/OTA 1190–1210 header over later alternatives without resolving the current scholarly disagreement.
openQuestions.question: Which proposed chronology best explains the poem's historical and textual evidence: the traditional 1189–1216 range or a later-thirteenth-century/post-1272 composition?

next_steps: Retain Nicholas only as a disputed attributed author unless new external evidence resolves the internal-reference problem.
Review competing authorship theories without turning the protagonist's proposed judge into the author by default.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's references to Nicholas of Guildford support an authorial attribution, or is anonymous authorship the only defensible cataloging position?

next_steps: Consult current dialectological and textual scholarship for evidence independent of either surviving scribe.
Keep catalog claims such as a probable Surrey or Dorset origin attributed to their sources until reconciled with the broader scholarship.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's place of composition be localized more narrowly than Southern England without conflating authorial origin with the Jesus witness's Ledbury-area scribal dialect?

assertion.notes: Southern origin is the broad consensus, but the linguistic and place-name evidence does not securely distinguish Surrey, Dorset, Wessex, the Home Counties, or the south-west Midlands.

notes: This shared localization concerns the poem's reconstructed original dialect and possible composition region; it is distinct from either surviving witness's scribal language.
This broad cultural-origin statement does not identify a precise place of composition.

assertion.notes: This localizes the surviving Jesus witness's scribal language, not necessarily the author's language or place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal language of the Jesus College MS 29 version

assertion.notes: Nicholas remains a plausible candidate, but the internal references do not prove authorship and alternate theories exist.

notes: Atkins edited and transcribed the witness; he is not the medieval author.

OTA combines the date 1922 with New York publisher Russell & Russell, whose matching reprint is dated 1971 by the Library of Congress. Exact electronic carrier dependence remains qualified in the lineage record.

scope.description: Underlying 1922 source edition represented by the OTA and U-M OwlJ encodings; the probable 1971 OTA carrier is modeled separately
Jesus College MS 29 text printed on the odd-numbered facing pages of the 1922 edition

assertion.notes: The poem draws on natural science, bestiary traditions, and beast-fable allusions without being reduced to a bestiary entry or beast fable.

scope.description: Closing appeal concerning Nicholas of Guildford, lines 1745–1778

assertion.notes: The poem sends the disputants to Nicholas of Guildford but ends before reporting a judgment.

summaries.value: An owl and a nightingale trade a long sequence of insults, defenses, exempla, and claims about beauty, song, usefulness, morality, love, marriage, religion, natural behavior, and social order. Their contest repeatedly adopts the language and tactics of formal pleading, but neither bird secures a verdict: they agree to take their dispute to Nicholas of Guildford, and the poem ends while they are still on their way. The comic, rhetorically agile irresolution keeps the values represented by both birds in play.

This record describes the complete Jesus College MS 29 textual version represented by OwlJ. The abstract work, its reconstructed lost common exemplar, the Cotton version, modern editions, digital encodings, and manuscript-image objects remain separate related or lineage entities.

The poem comprises 1,794 verse lines and a separate opening Latin rubric. The repository XML's eighteen supplied 100-line DIV1 groups (the last holding 94 lines), odd-page PB markers, and folio/column locators are encoding and edition navigation, not intrinsic medieval sections. Five expected old-foliation column labels survive as literal text within L elements rather than as MILESTONE elements; together with 47 MILESTONE elements they cover the legacy sequence 229r.1–241v.2.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical distinguishes the cataloged Jesus-witness version from the abstract work and the Cotton version.

titles.value: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)

notes: This conventional modern title belongs to the abstract work and its editions; it is not the Latin rubric written in the Jesus manuscript.

titles.value: The owl and the nightingale (MS Jes. Col. 29)

notes: A scribal rubric meaning that the debate between the nightingale and owl begins; it is not treated as an authorial title or one of the poem's 1,794 verse lines.

scope.description: Opening rubric in the Jesus witness

titles.value: Incipit altercacio inter filomenam et Bubonem
Ich wes in one sumere dale / In one swiþe dyele hale
Ne can ic eu namore telle; / Her nys namore of þisse spelle.

work.summary: An owl and a nightingale trade a long sequence of insults, defenses, exempla, and claims about beauty, song, usefulness, morality, love, marriage, religion, natural behavior, and social order. Their contest repeatedly adopts the language and tactics of formal pleading, but neither bird secures a verdict: they agree to take their dispute to Nicholas of Guildford, and the poem ends while they are still on their way. The comic, rhetorically agile irresolution keeps the values represented by both birds in play.
